@-webkit-keyframes progress-bar-stripes {
  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}

  from {
    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}

    background-position: 40px 0;
  }
  to {
    background-position: 0 0;
  }
}
.progress {
  height: 20px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  overflow: hidden;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, .1);

  float: left;
  width: 0;
  height: 100%;

  color: #fff;

  background-color: #337ab7;

  background-image:         linear-gradient(45deg, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 25%, transparent 25%, transparent 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 50%, rgba(255, 255, 255, .15) 75%, transparent 75%, transparent);
  -webkit-background-size: 40px 40px;
          background-size: 40px 40px;
}

.embed-responsive {
  position: relative;
  display: block;
  height: 0;
  padding: 0;
  overflow: hidden;
}

.embed-responsive iframe,
.embed-responsive embed,
.embed-responsive object,
.embed-responsive video {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;

  left: 0;
  width: 100%;
  height: 100%;
  border: 0;
}
.embed-responsive-16by9 {
  padding-bottom: 56.25%;
}
.embed-responsive-4by3 {
  padding-bottom: 75%;

.well {
  min-height: 20px;
  padding: 19px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border: 1px solid #e3e3e3;
  border-radius: 4px;
  -webkit-box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);
          box-shadow: inset 0 1px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, .05);

  padding: 24px;
  border-radius: 6px;
}

  padding: 9px;
  border-radius: 3px;

.close {

  font-size: 21px;
  font-weight: bold;

  opacity: .2;

.close:focus {

  cursor: pointer;
  filter: alpha(opacity=50);
  opacity: .5;
}

<h1>electronics</h1>
<h5>Review</h5>
<h2>A Survey of Intelligent End-to-End Networking Solutions:</h2>
<h2>Integrating Graph Neural Networks and Deep Reinforcement</h2>
<h2>Learning Approaches</h2>
<h5>Prohim Tam^1 , Seyha Ros^1 , Inseok Song^1 , Seungwoo Kang^1 and Seokhoon Kim1,2,*</h5>
<p>(^1) Department of Software Convergence, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea;<br>
prohimtam@gmail.com (P.T.); rosseyha003@gmail.com (S.R.); sis5041@sch.ac.kr (I.S.);<br>
oooksw12@sch.ac.kr (S.K.)<br>
(^2) Department of Computer Software Engineering, Soonchunhyang University, Asan 31538, Republic of Korea<br>
***** Correspondence: seokhoon@sch.ac.kr<br>
<strong>Abstract:</strong> This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the integration of graph neural networks<br>
(GNN) and deep reinforcement learning (DRL) in end-to-end (E2E) networking solutions. We delve<br>
into the fundamentals of GNN, its variants, and the state-of-the-art applications in communication<br>
networking, which reveal the potential to revolutionize access, transport, and core network manage-<br>
ment policies. This paper further explores DRL capabilities, its variants, and the trending applications<br>
in E2E networking, particularly in enhancing dynamic network (re)configurations and resource<br>
management. By fusing GNN with DRL, we spotlight novel approaches, ranging from radio access<br>
networks to core management and orchestration, across E2E network layers. Deployment scenarios<br>
in smart transportation, smart factory, and smart grids demonstrate the practical implications of our<br>
survey topic. Lastly, we point out potential challenges and future research directions, including the<br>
critical aspects for modelling explainability, the reduction in overhead consumption, interoperability<br>
with existing schemes, and the importance of reproducibility. Our survey aims to serve as a roadmap<br>
for future developments in E2E networking, guiding through the current landscape, challenges,<br>
and prospective breakthroughs in the algorithm modelling toward network automation using GNN<br>
and DRL.<br>
<strong>Keywords:</strong> deep reinforcement learning; end-to-end networking; graph neural networks; network<br>
automation; optimization approaches</p>
<h5>1. Introduction</h5>
<h5>Following the establishment of comprehensive advanced 5G and 6G standards, 2019</h5>
<h5>to 2023 has witnessed the pioneering commercial deployment of fast-speed wireless net-</h5>
<h5>works, which supports the advent of smart digital transformation. The internet evolution</h5>
<h5>presents advancements in ultra-reliable low-latency, high-throughput, mobility-aware, and</h5>
<h5>high-coverage connectivity that set a new benchmark compared to the previous network</h5>
<h5>generations [ 1 , 2 ]. Forecasts by the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) anticipate</h5>
<h5>exponential growth in global mobile data traffic, with projections extending from 390 ex-</h5>
<h5>abytes to 5016 exabytes between 2024 and 2030, respectively [ 3 ]. Figure 1 presents the ITU</h5>
<h5>prediction outputs. As digital transformation and its volume expand with the benefits of</h5>
<h5>widespread coverage and lightning-fast connections, it also faces significant challenges</h5>
<h5>in managing the growth in data, devices, and services [ 4 , 5 ]. To address these evolving</h5>
<h5>challenges, a shift towards network automation is essential to breaking down barriers</h5>
<h5>within end-to-end (E2E) solutions, which spans three domains: radio access networks</h5>
<h5>(RAN), transport networks, and core networks. There are several ways of categorizing</h5>
<h5>the domains; however, we prioritize the functionality and specifications from research</h5>
<p>Electronics <strong>2024</strong> , 13 , 994. https://doi.org/10.3390/electronics13050994 https://www.mdpi.com/journal/electronics</p>
<pre><code>Electronics 2024 , 13 , 994 2 of 29
</code></pre>
<h5>perspectives, whether dealing with radio aspects, transmission of data, or critical network</h5>
<h5>functions, in order to support independent technological advancements.</h5>
<h5>Electronics 2024 , 13 , x FOR PEER REVIEW 29 of 29</h5>
<h3>research perspectives, whether dealing with radio aspects, transmission of data, or critical</h3>
<h3>network functions, in order to support independent technological advancements.</h3>
<h3>Traditional RAN requires redesigning with AI-empowered control [6], shared cloud-</h3>
<h3>ification [7], optimized power allocation [8,9], and highly programmable handover and</h3>
<h3>interoperability [10]. During the redesign process, initial challenges arise in data exposure</h3>
<h3>capability and the level of network infrastructure knowledge necessary to support rich-</h3>
<h3>feature input and processing for network automation. Considering the significant objec-</h3>
<h3>tives of integrating AI, O-RAN, and software-defined networking (SDN)-enabled man-</h3>
<h3>agement, the ability to encode network conditions (signal, interference, spectrum availa-</h3>
<h3>bility, etc.) and decode hidden relationships between each timeslot remains burdensome.</h3>
<h3>Furthermore, transport and core networks also require the ability to understand traffic</h3>
<h3>(congestion) patterns, resource utilization, and anomaly detection in complex topology</h3>
<h3>graphs [11–13]. Therefore, before focusing on other potential issues in E2E networking,</h3>
<h3>one key research is the selection of optimization algorithms that handle complex graph-</h3>
<h3>structured topologies and extract data to support self-organizing capabilities [14,15].</h3>
<h4>Figure 1. Predictions by ITU on mobile data traffic growth.</h4>
<h3>Previous works supported by standardization, academia, and industry experts, are</h3>
<h3>coming to conduct the creation of cutting-edge testbeds and simulation tools for network</h3>
<h3>intelligence [16–19]. The motivation from existing testbeds has guided researchers to-</h3>
<h3>wards integrating three key objectives, namely zero-touch autonomy, topology-aware</h3>
<h3>scalability, and long-term efficiency, into network and service management [20,21]. In</h3>
<h3>terms of these goal-oriented optimizations, graph neural networks (GNN) [22–24] and</h3>
<h3>deep reinforcement learning (DRL) [25–27] are at the forefront of algorithms for advancing</h3>
<h3>network automation with capabilities of extracting features and multi-aspect awareness</h3>
<h3>in building controller policies. While GNN offers non-Euclidean topology awareness, fea-</h3>
<h3>ture learning on graphs, generalization, representation learning, permutation equivari-</h3>
<h3>ance, and propagation analysis [28–31], it lacks capabilities in continuous optimization</h3>
<h3>and long-term exploration/exploitation strategies. Therefore, DRL is an optimal comple-</h3>
<h3>ment to GNN, enhancing the applications towards achieving specific policies within the</h3>
<h3>scope of E2E network automation.</h3>
<h3>Building upon the backgrounds, challenges, and motivations mentioned above, we</h3>
<h3>have compiled a comprehensive review of existing works on GNN+DRL from a</h3>
<pre><code>Figure 1. Predictions by ITU on mobile data traffic growth.
</code></pre>
<h5>Traditional RAN requires redesigning with AI-empowered control [ 6 ], shared cloud-</h5>
<h5>ification [ 7 ], optimized power allocation [ 8 , 9 ], and highly programmable handover and</h5>
<h5>interoperability [ 10 ]. During the redesign process, initial challenges arise in data exposure</h5>
<h5>capability and the level of network infrastructure knowledge necessary to support rich-</h5>
<h5>feature input and processing for network automation. Considering the significant objectives</h5>
<h5>of integrating AI, O-RAN, and software-defined networking (SDN)-enabled management,</h5>
<h5>the ability to encode network conditions (signal, interference, spectrum availability, etc.)</h5>
<h5>and decode hidden relationships between each timeslot remains burdensome. Furthermore,</h5>
<h5>transport and core networks also require the ability to understand traffic (congestion)</h5>
<h5>patterns, resource utilization, and anomaly detection in complex topology graphs [ 11 – 13 ].</h5>
<h5>Therefore, before focusing on other potential issues in E2E networking, one key research is</h5>
<h5>the selection of optimization algorithms that handle complex graph-structured topologies</h5>
<h5>and extract data to support self-organizing capabilities [14,15].</h5>
<h5>Previous works supported by standardization, academia, and industry experts, are</h5>
<h5>coming to conduct the creation of cutting-edge testbeds and simulation tools for network</h5>
<h5>intelligence [ 16 – 19 ]. The motivation from existing testbeds has guided researchers towards</h5>
<h5>integrating three key objectives, namely zero-touch autonomy, topology-aware scalability,</h5>
<h5>and long-term efficiency, into network and service management [ 20 , 21 ]. In terms of these</h5>
<h5>goal-oriented optimizations, graph neural networks (GNN) [ 22 – 24 ] and deep reinforce-</h5>
<h5>ment learning (DRL) [ 25 – 27 ] are at the forefront of algorithms for advancing network</h5>
<h5>automation with capabilities of extracting features and multi-aspect awareness in building</h5>
<h5>controller policies. While GNN offers non-Euclidean topology awareness, feature learning</h5>
<h5>on graphs, generalization, representation learning, permutation equivariance, and prop-</h5>
<h5>agation analysis [ 28 – 31 ], it lacks capabilities in continuous optimization and long-term</h5>
<h5>exploration/exploitation strategies. Therefore, DRL is an optimal complement to GNN,</h5>
<h5>enhancing the applications towards achieving specific policies within the scope of E2E</h5>
<h5>network automation.</h5>
<h5>Building upon the backgrounds, challenges, and motivations mentioned above, we</h5>
<h5>have compiled a comprehensive review of existing works on GNN+DRL from a commu-</h5>
<h5>nications perspective. Table 1 presents our targeted comparison and the contributions of</h5>
<h5>previous notable literature reviews, as well as how our work adds novelty to this research</h5>
<h5>domain by emphasizing the integration modelling of GNN+DRL algorithms.</h5>

<h5>In our review, we gathered papers from search engines, primarily using Google Scholar,</h5>
<h5>with keywords combining of DRL or/and GNN for searching in networking key terms</h5>
<h5>such as routing optimization, resource management, energy efficiency, access networks,</h5>
<h5>core networks, and transport networks. We found more than 60 papers were published</h5>
<h5>between January 2017 and January 2024. Then, we filtered the key articles (integrating</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL) to conduct the review in the main section. The remaining articles are analyzed</h5>
<h5>for preliminary sections. Figure 2 presents the paper structure.</h5>

<h5>In our review, we gathered papers from search engines, primarily using Google</h5>
<h5>Scholar, with keywords combining of DRL or/and GNN for searching in networking key</h5>
<h5>terms such as routing optimization, resource management, energy efficiency, access net-</h5>
<h5>works, core networks, and transport networks. We found more than 60 papers were pub-</h5>
<h5>lished between January 2017 and January 2024. Then, we filtered the key articles (integrat-</h5>
<h5>ing GNN+DRL) to conduct the review in the main section. The remaining articles are an-</h5>
<h5>alyzed for preliminary sections. Figure 2 presents the paper structure.</h5>
<pre><code>Figure 2. Paper structure.
</code></pre>
<h5>Our contributions primarily stand with the applications of GNN+DRL in three net-</h5>
<h5>work domains across access, transport, and core layers. Furthermore, we review the de-</h5>
<h5>ployment strategies in three use cases, namely smart transportation, smart factory, and</h5>
<h5>smart grids. Given that both GNN+DRL are in the early stages of development and are</h5>
<h5>primarily explored through theoretical research perspectives, we aim to point out the po-</h5>
<h5>tential challenges and future directions. Our review targets understanding the current</h5>
<h5>limitations and envisioning the roadmap for advancing these paradigm integrations in</h5>
<h5>practical applications and innovative solutions for (future) massive data traffic.</h5>
<pre><code>Figure 2. Paper structure.
</code></pre>
<h5>Our contributions primarily stand with the applications of GNN+DRL in three network</h5>
<h5>domains across access, transport, and core layers. Furthermore, we review the deployment</h5>
<h5>strategies in three use cases, namely smart transportation, smart factory, and smart grids.</h5>
<h5>Given that both GNN+DRL are in the early stages of development and are primarily</h5>
<h5>explored through theoretical research perspectives, we aim to point out the potential</h5>
<h5>challenges and future directions. Our review targets understanding the current limitations</h5>
<h5>and envisioning the roadmap for advancing these paradigm integrations in practical</h5>
<h5>applications and innovative solutions for (future) massive data traffic.</h5>
<h5>Our review is structured as follows. Sections 2 and 3 present the background studies</h5>
<h5>of both GNN and DRL (its variants and applications). Section 4 provides the integrated</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL approaches for E2E networking solutions. Section 5 showcases the deployment</h5>
<h5>scenarios in smart applications. Section 6 highlights the research challenges and future</h5>
<h5>research directions. Finally, Section 7 concludes our literature summary. Table 2 presents</h5>
<h5>the key acronyms used in this paper.</h5>

<h5>2. Preliminary on GNN</h5>
<h5>2.1. GNN and Its Variants</h5>
<h5>GNN represents a class of deep learning models designed to perform inference on</h5>
<h5>data structured as graphs. Initially, GNN is particularly powerful for tasks where the</h5>
<h5>data are inherently graph structured, such as social networks [ 32 ], chemistry [ 33 ], and</h5>
<h5>communication networks [ 34 ]. The core idea behind GNN is to learn representations</h5>
<h5>(embeddings) for each node/edge that capture both (1) key features and (2) the structure</h5>
<h5>of local graph neighborhood. GNN iteratively updates the representation of a node by</h5>
<h5>aggregating representations of its neighboring nodes and combining them with its current</h5>
<h5>representation. This message-passing process involves two main steps:</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Aggregation</strong> : Each node aggregates its own features with those of its neighbors,</li>
</ul>
<h5>creating a unified vector that represents the local network structure. Equation (1)</h5>
<h5>presents an overview of aggregating information from neighbor nodes ofi, denoted as</h5>
<h5>a</h5>
<pre><code>(l+ 1 )
</code></pre>
<h5>i , where (1)h</h5>
<pre><code>(l)
</code></pre>
<h5>j is the feature vector of nodejat layerl, and (2)j ε N(i)denotes</h5>
<h5>the set of connected neighbors.</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Update</strong> : The representation of each node is updated by combining its current repre-</li>
</ul>
<h5>sentation with the aggregated neighboring node representation (often using a neural</h5>
<h5>network). Equation (2) presents a general GNN layer for combiningh(il)anda(il+^1 )</h5>
<h5>towards next-layer featuresh(il+^1 ).</h5>
<h5>a(il+^1 )=AGGREGATE(l)</h5>

i

<h5></h5>
<h5>(2)</h5>
<h5>Several well-known variants of GNNs have been developed, where each with its own</h5>
<h5>approach to modify on aggregation and updating steps, including (1) graph convolutional</h5>
<h5>networks (GCN) [ 35 ] simplify the aggregation step by using a weighted average of neighbor</h5>
<h5>features, where weights are typically based on the degree of the nodes; (2) graph attention</h5>
<h5>networks (GAT) [ 36 ] introduce attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of each</h5>
<h5>neighbor’s features during aggregation dynamically; (3) GraphSAGE [ 37 ] extend GNN by</h5>
<h5>sampling a fixed-size neighborhood for each node and using various aggregation functions,</h5>
<h5>such as mean, LSTM, or pooling; (4) message passing neural networks (MPNN) [ 38 ]</h5>
<h5>generalize several GNN models by defining a message passing framework, where messages</h5>
<h5>(aggregated features) are passed between nodes; (5) edge-node GNN [ 39 ] target on edge</h5>
<h5>updates alongside node updates for radio resource management, which demonstrated</h5>
<h5>superior performance in beamforming and power allocation to achieve higher rates with</h5>
<h5>less computation time.</h5>
<h5>2.2. Applied GNN in E2E Networking</h5>
<h5>Beyond traditional networking approaches, GNN offers a paradigm shift for network</h5>
<h5>intelligence through the capability to model and analyze the hidden relationships and</h5>
<h5>dynamic attributes in graph-structured massive network topologies. Furthermore, GNN</h5>
<h5>with permutation equivariance offers a significant advantage in communication networks</h5>
<h5>by treating equivalent network configurations, even if nodes swap positions, as the same</h5>
<h5>from a network function perspective. This key factor translates to reduced training effort,</h5>
<h5>making GNN particularly well suited for analyzing and optimizing complex network</h5>
<h5>structures [ 39 , 40 ]. Table 3 presents an overview of the selected GNN implementation in</h5>
<h5>E2E networking. We focus on (1) specifying the networking domains addressed by the</h5>
<h5>authors, which span from access to core network optimization policies, (2) pointing out the</h5>
<h5>primary input graphs and selected features, (3) describing the processing methodology of</h5>
<h5>the proposed (variant) GNN models, and (4) identifying the types of readout (either flow</h5>
<h5>level, node-level or graph-level prediction) with the target output.</h5>
<pre><code>Table 3. Selected comprehensive works on applied GNN.
</code></pre>

<h5>3. Preliminary on DRL</h5>
<h5>3.1. DRL and Its Variants</h5>
<h5>DRL combines the principles of reinforcement learning with the representation learn-</h5>
<h5>ing capabilities of deep neural networks (DNN) by (1) enabling agents to learn optimal</h5>
<h5>policies for decision making, (2) interacting with the environment through observing states</h5>
<h5>and applying actions, (3) receiving feedback by proposing specific reward functions, and (4)</h5>
<h5>targeting to maximize cumulative long-term rewards [ 47 ]. The foundations of DRL involve</h5>
<h5>the Bellman equation used to update the value, as Equations (3) and (4), where (1)V(s)is</h5>
<h5>the value of states, (2)Q(s,a)is the value of taking actionain states, (3)Rtis the reward</h5>
<h5>at timet, and (4) γ is the discount factor.</h5>
<h5>V(s)=E[Rt+ γ V(st+ 1 )|st=s] (3)</h5>
<h5>Q(s,a)=E</h5>
<h5></h5>
<h5>Rt+ γ max</h5>
<pre><code>a′
</code></pre>
<h5>Q</h5>
<h5></h5>
<h5>st+ 1 ,a′</h5>
<h5></h5>
<h5>st=s, at=a</h5>
<h5></h5>
<h5>(4)</h5>
<h5>Several algorithms and architectures have been developed in DRL to address different</h5>
<h5>challenges, including (1) deep Q-networks (DQN) [ 48 ] learns the action-value function with</h5>
<h5>DNN and stabilizes Q-learning by using experience replay with fixed Q-targets, (2) policy</h5>
<h5>gradient methods (e.g., REINFORCE, actor-critic) [ 49 ] directly learns the policy function</h5>
<h5>while potentially also learning a value function to assist in the learning process, (3) proximal</h5>
<h5>policy optimization (PPO) [ 50 ] improves the stability and efficiency of policy gradient</h5>
<h5>methods with techniques that limit the updates to the policy, and (4) deep deterministic</h5>
<h5>policy gradient (DDPG) [ 51 ] combines the ideas of Q-learning with policy gradients to</h5>
<h5>handle continuous action spaces.</h5>
<h5>3.2. Applied DRL in E2E Networking</h5>
<h5>DRL marks a significant evolution in networking intelligence, diverging from con-</h5>
<h5>ventional strategies by its adaptability and learning-driven approach to optimize network</h5>
<h5>functions [ 52 – 55 ]. Table 4 outlines DRL notable studies in E2E networking contexts, includ-</h5>
<h5>ing the networking domains, key remarks, state observation, action implementation, and</h5>
<h5>reward targets.</h5>

<h5>4. Integrated GNN and DRL in E2E Networking Solutions</h5>
<h5>The synergy of GNN and DRL capitalizes on (1) GNN : the capability to encode</h5>
<h5>complex graph environments, approximate actions/rewards, and compute q-values, along</h5>
<h5>with (2) DRL : the ability to explore GNN architectures and evaluate the accuracy of readout</h5>
<h5>predictions. Figure 3 presents the overview of fusing both algorithms and key features that</h5>
<h5>complement each other. Together, GNN+DRL extract auxiliary network states, advance</h5>
<h5>generalization/adaptability, and adopt data-driven learning for multi-aspect awareness</h5>
<h5>reward functions towards pioneering network automation.</h5>

<h5>4.1. Access Networks</h5>
<h5>In this sub-section, we outline the primary contributions to access network policies,</h5>
<h5>focusing on the integration of network topologies as comprehensive graphs for early pro-</h5>
<h5>cessing. This approach targets readout objectives through continuous learning capabilities</h5>
<h5>and non-Euclidean feature extraction. Figure 4 illustrates the schematic representation of</h5>
<h5>the wireless network input in relation to the policy objectives that emphasize the strategic</h5>
<h5>applications of integrating GNN and DRL. The key to understanding how GNN works is</h5>
<h5>focusing on how graph information is input to subsequent hidden layers, which primarily</h5>
<h5>involves the concepts of message passing, aggregation, feature transformation, and update</h5>
<h5>mechanisms that enable the network to learn from the graph structures and node features.</h5>
<h5>After the initial round, the updated node features can serve as input to the next hidden</h5>
<h5>layers. Each hidden layer can perform its own steps, which allows the network to capture</h5>
<h5>more complex patterns and relationships at higher levels of abstraction. The depth of the</h5>
<h5>network (number of hidden layers) typically correlates with the reach of a node (e.g., how</h5>
<h5>many hops away in the graph the node information can propagate from).</h5>
<h5>Electronics 2024 , 13 , x FOR PEER REVIEW 29 of 29</h5>
<h2>4.1. Access Networks</h2>
<h2>In this sub-section, we outline the primary contributions to access network policies,</h2>
<h2>focusing on the integration of network topologies as comprehensive graphs for early pro-</h2>
<h2>cessing. This approach targets readout objectives through continuous learning capabilities</h2>
<h2>and non-Euclidean feature extraction. Figure 4 illustrates the schematic representation of</h2>
<h2>the wireless network input in relation to the policy objectives that emphasize the strategic</h2>
<h2>applications of integrating GNN and DRL. The key to understanding how GNN works is</h2>
<h2>focusing on how graph information is input to subsequent hidden layers, which primarily</h2>
<h2>involves the concepts of message passing, aggregation, feature transformation, and up-</h2>
<h2>date mechanisms that enable the network to learn from the graph structures and node</h2>
<h2>features. After the initial round, the updated node features can serve as input to the next</h2>
<h2>hidden layers. Each hidden layer can perform its own steps, which allows the network to</h2>
<h2>capture more complex patterns and relationships at higher levels of abstraction. The depth</h2>
<h2>of the network (number of hidden layers) typically correlates with the reach of a node</h2>
<h2>(e.g., how many hops away in the graph the node information can propagate from).</h2>
<h3>Figure 4. Schematic graph processing from input network graphs towards access network policies.</h3>
<h2>4.1.1. RAN Slicing</h2>
<h2>Arash et al. [62] proposed a GNN-based multi-agent DRL framework for RAN/mo-</h2>
<h2>bile edge computing (MEC) slicing and admission control in 5G metropolitan networks.</h2>
<h2>The authors leveraged GAT and GATv2 for topology-independent feature extraction,</h2>
<h2>which enabled scalability and generalizability across different networks. The approach</h2>
<h2>used multi-agent DRL, combining a GNN-based slicing agent with a topology-independ-</h2>
<h2>ent multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for admission control, for optimizing long-term revenue</h2>
<h2>under E2E service delay and resource constraints. The framework demonstrated signifi-</h2>
<h2>cant improvements in infrastructure provider’s revenue, achieving up to 35.2% and 25.5%</h2>
<h2>overall gain over other DRL-based and heuristic baselines. The proposed scheme main-</h2>
<h2>tained good performances without re-training or re-tuning, even when applied to unseen</h2>
<h2>network topologies, which showcased its generalizability and robustness.</h2>
<h2>4.1.2. Radio Resource Allocation</h2>
<h2>In E2E solutions, efficient radio resource allocation is crucial for optimal service de-</h2>
<h2>livery in ensuring fairness, quality of service (QoS), efficiency, and cost-effectiveness in</h2>
<h2>operational expenses. Zhao et al. [63] introduced graph reinforcement learning by first</h2>
<h2>transforming the traditional state and action representations from matrices to graphs,</h2>
<h2>which enabled the functionality of GNN in capturing graph-structured network topolo-</h2>
<h2>gies and node-level relationships efficiently. The graph-based representation was then uti-</h2>
<h2>lized within a DDPG framework, where the actor and critic networks were adapted to</h2>
<h2>handle graph inputs to allow the model to learn optimal policies for resource allocation.</h2>
<pre><code>Figure 4. Schematic graph processing from input network graphs towards access network policies.
</code></pre>
<h5>4.1.1. RAN Slicing</h5>
<h5>Arash et al. [ 62 ] proposed a GNN-based multi-agent DRL framework for RAN/mobile</h5>
<h5>edge computing (MEC) slicing and admission control in 5G metropolitan networks. The</h5>
<h5>authors leveraged GAT and GATv2 for topology-independent feature extraction, which</h5>
<h5>enabled scalability and generalizability across different networks. The approach used</h5>
<h5>multi-agent DRL, combining a GNN-based slicing agent with a topology-independent</h5>
<h5>multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for admission control, for optimizing long-term revenue</h5>
<h5>under E2E service delay and resource constraints. The framework demonstrated significant</h5>
<h5>improvements in infrastructure provider ’s revenue, achieving up to 35.2% and 25.5% over-</h5>
<h5>all gain over other DRL-based and heuristic baselines. The proposed scheme maintained</h5>
<h5>good performances without re-training or re-tuning, even when applied to unseen network</h5>
<h5>topologies, which showcased its generalizability and robustness.</h5>
<h5>4.1.2. Radio Resource Allocation</h5>
<h5>In E2E solutions, efficient radio resource allocation is crucial for optimal service de-</h5>
<h5>livery in ensuring fairness, quality of service (QoS), efficiency, and cost-effectiveness in</h5>
<h5>operational expenses. Zhao et al. [ 63 ] introduced graph reinforcement learning by first</h5>
<h5>transforming the traditional state and action representations from matrices to graphs,</h5>
<h5>which enabled the functionality of GNN in capturing graph-structured network topologies</h5>
<h5>and node-level relationships efficiently. The graph-based representation was then utilized</h5>
<h5>within a DDPG framework, where the actor and critic networks were adapted to handle</h5>
<h5>graph inputs to allow the model to learn optimal policies for resource allocation. The pro-</h5>
<h5>posed approach not only reduced the dimensionality of the input data but also captured the</h5>
<h5>relational dynamics between network elements more effectively than traditional methods.</h5>
<h5>The results showcased significant improvements in training efficiency and performance for</h5>
<h5>radio resource allocation tasks. The graph-based DDPG algorithm demonstrated faster con-</h5>
<h5>vergence, lesser computing resource consumption, and lower space complexity compared</h5>
<h5>to traditional DDPG algorithms.</h5>
<h5>Furthermore, Yuan et al. [ 64 ] focused on the dynamic assignment of spectrum and</h5>
<h5>power resources in a cognitive radio network that uses both overlay (where secondary</h5>
<h5>users utilize spectrum not used by primary users) and underlay (where secondary users</h5>
<h5>share spectrum with primary users under certain interference constraints) access methods,</h5>
<h5>enhanced by network slicing to support different QoS service requirements. The integrated</h5>
<h5>scheme, termed graph convolution reinforcement learning algorithm, leveraged GCN for</h5>
<h5>agents to efficiently gather and utilize both personal and neighboring information that</h5>
<h5>enhances local agent collaboration. The proposed method enhanced the cognitive network’s</h5>
<h5>overall power efficiency.</h5>
<h5>Beyond this, Zhao et al. [ 65 ] utilized GCN for extracting interference features from the</h5>
<h5>graph and combining the features (user distance distribution and resource states) with a</h5>
<h5>DRL approach for decision making in channel state information estimation. The E2E model</h5>
<h5>integrated feature extraction and policy generation. The learning process was guided by a</h5>
<h5>policy gradient method for optimizing channel selection and power adaptation actions to</h5>
<h5>improve spectrum sharing and mitigate interference.</h5>
<h5>4.1.3. User Association</h5>
<h5>Ibtihal et al. [ 66 ] proposed DQN-GNN processing flow for optimizing user association</h5>
<h5>in wireless networks involves a sequence of steps. Initially, the system represents the</h5>
<h5>user association problem as a graph, where nodes correspond to users or base stations</h5>
<h5>(BS), and edges represent wireless connections. A GNN is then used to encode this graph</h5>
<h5>structure by learning a representation for each node to understand the importance and</h5>
<h5>connectivity within the network. Following these steps, a DQN agent is trained to decide</h5>
<h5>the best base station for user connection based on the network state, which includes</h5>
<h5>user–BS associations and other network parameters. The integration of GNN with DQN</h5>
<h5>leverages the encoded graph structure to inform the DQN agent decisions, which aims to</h5>
<h5>optimize network performance by selecting the optimal user–BS associations to maximize</h5>
<h5>the reward evaluation. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed GNN-DQN approach</h5>
<h5>is tested and evaluated against other user association methods to showcase its ability in</h5>
<h5>adapting and providing efficient solutions in dynamic wireless network environments.</h5>
<h5>Figure 5 shows the layer structure between cognitive radio networks to agent controller in</h5>
<h5>building the graph, mapping, and finalizing the policy control for user association.</h5>
<p>Electronics <em>Electronics</em> <strong>20242024</strong> ,,^13 13 , 994, x FOR PEER REVIEW 29 of 29 15 of 29</p>
<pre><code>Figure 5. Agent (central controller) for user association control.
</code></pre>
<h5>4.1.4. Cluster-Free NOMA</h5>
<h5>A NOMA framework is designed to enhance the flexibility of successive interference</h5>
<h5>cancellation operations, which eliminates the need for user clustering. The cluster-free ob-</h5>
<h5>jective aims to efficiently mitigate interference and improve system performance by ena-</h5>
<h5>bling more adaptable and scenario-responsive NOMA communications. Xu et al. [67] pro-</h5>
<h5>posed a comprehensive framework that significantly increases the flexibility of successive</h5>
<h5>interference cancellation operations, which is supported by advanced DRL with GNN</h5>
<h5>paradigms (automated learning GNN termed as AutoGNN) to achieve scenario-adaptive</h5>
<h5>and efficient communications in next-generation multiple access environments. The pro-</h5>
<h5>posed algorithm leveraged the GNN+DRL integration to minimize interference and opti-</h5>
<h5>mize beamforming in a flexible flow for cluster-free NOMA setting. The results high-</h5>
<h5>lighted that the proposed AutoGNN approach for cluster-free NOMA can outperform</h5>
<h5>conventional cluster-based NOMA across various channel correlations. Notably, while</h5>
<h5>unsupervised centralized convolutional neural networks yield the lowest performance</h5>
<h5>due to the non-structural nature and scalability issues, the structural GNNs achieve sys-</h5>
<h5>tem sum rates comparable to centralized optimization methods. AutoGNN achieved</h5>
<h5>higher system sum rates than both the cluster-free NOMA with (1) centralized/distributed</h5>
<h5>alternating direction method of multipliers and (2) the centralized convolutional neural</h5>
<h5>networks approach. Specifically, the system sum rate (bps/Hz) for cluster-free NOMA</h5>
<h5>with AutoGNN is highly efficient, which indicated a better performance enhancement.</h5>
<h5>4.2. Transport Networks</h5>

<h5>Swaminatha et al. [68] proposed GraphNET approach by integrating GNN with DRL</h5>

<h5>lays. The GNN+DRL algorithm significantly reduced packet drops and achieved lower</h5>
<h5>average delays compared to traditional Q-routing and shortest path algorithms. Figure 6</h5>
<h5>illustrates the interactions between SDN architecture and GNN+DRL by offering (1) state</h5>

</code></pre>
<h5>4.1.4. Cluster-Free NOMA</h5>
<h5>A NOMA framework is designed to enhance the flexibility of successive interference</h5>
<h5>cancellation operations, which eliminates the need for user clustering. The cluster-free</h5>
<h5>objective aims to efficiently mitigate interference and improve system performance by</h5>
<h5>enabling more adaptable and scenario-responsive NOMA communications. Xu et al. [ 67 ]</h5>
<h5>proposed a comprehensive framework that significantly increases the flexibility of suc-</h5>
<h5>cessive interference cancellation operations, which is supported by advanced DRL with</h5>
<h5>GNN paradigms (automated learning GNN termed as AutoGNN) to achieve scenario-</h5>
<h5>adaptive and efficient communications in next-generation multiple access environments.</h5>
<h5>The proposed algorithm leveraged the GNN+DRL integration to minimize interference</h5>
<h5>and optimize beamforming in a flexible flow for cluster-free NOMA setting. The results</h5>
<h5>highlighted that the proposed AutoGNN approach for cluster-free NOMA can outperform</h5>
<h5>conventional cluster-based NOMA across various channel correlations. Notably, while</h5>
<h5>unsupervised centralized convolutional neural networks yield the lowest performance due</h5>
<h5>to the non-structural nature and scalability issues, the structural GNNs achieve system sum</h5>
<h5>rates comparable to centralized optimization methods. AutoGNN achieved higher system</h5>
<h5>sum rates than both the cluster-free NOMA with (1) centralized/distributed alternating</h5>
<h5>direction method of multipliers and (2) the centralized convolutional neural networks ap-</h5>
<h5>proach. Specifically, the system sum rate (bps/Hz) for cluster-free NOMA with AutoGNN</h5>
<h5>is highly efficient, which indicated a better performance enhancement.</h5>
<h5>4.2. Transport Networks</h5>
<h5>4.2.1. Routing Optimization</h5>
<h5>Swaminatha et al. [ 68 ] proposed GraphNET approach by integrating GNN with DRL</h5>
<h5>frameworks to optimize routing decisions in SDN. There are two primary phases, namely</h5>
<h5>inference and training. Initially, a network state matrix synchronized with the proposed</h5>
<h5>GNN, which then predicts the most optimal path with minimal delay. The GNN, acting</h5>
<h5>as a DQN within the DRL framework, is trained using experiencing routing episodes,</h5>
<h5>which employs a custom reward function focused on packet delivery and minimizing</h5>
<h5>delays. The GNN+DRL algorithm significantly reduced packet drops and achieved lower</h5>
<h5>average delays compared to traditional Q-routing and shortest path algorithms. Figure 6</h5>
<h5>illustrates the interactions between SDN architecture and GNN+DRL by offering (1) state</h5>
<h5>observability by SDN interfaces, (2) computability on SDN databases/controllers, and</h5>
<h5>(3) action configurations translate to SDN forwarding rules.</h5>

<h5>Furthermore, He et al. [69], leveraged knowledge-defined networking architecture</h5>
<h5>for machine learning programmability to transform network data into actionable</h5>
<h5>knowledge that intelligently guides routing policies for improved network management.</h5>
<h5>The proposed integration, message passing DRL (MPDRL) architecture, utilized GNN and</h5>
<h5>DDPG to address routing optimization by (1) GNN interacts with the network topology</h5>
<h5>to extract knowledge through message passing, while (2) DDPG utilizes the hidden</h5>
<h5>knowledge to generate optimal routing policies. In this study, authors optimized the com-</h5>
<h5>bination for enhancing network traffic load balancing and overall performance by dynam-</h5>
<h5>ically adapting to network changes. Through extensive experiments across three real-</h5>
<h5>world internet service provider (ISP) network topologies, the proposed routing optimiza-</h5>
<h5>tion method consistently achieved better network performance metrics, such as reducing</h5>
<h5>maximum link utilization, decreasing E2E network delay, and enhancing overall network</h5>
<h5>utility compared to (1) open shortest path first, (2) equal-cost multi-path, and (3) the orig-</h5>
<h5>inal DDPG methods.</h5>
<h5>4.2.2. Flow Migration</h5>
<h5>Sun et al. [70] proposed an optimization approach on flow migration, which referred</h5>
<h5>to the dynamic relocation of traffic among different network function instances to adapt</h5>
<h5>the loading statuses and balancing between network service quality and resource utiliza-</h5>
<h5>tion efficiency. The proposed framework was termed DeepMigration, which utilized (1)</h5>
<h5>GNN to handle graph-structured topology and flow distribution and (2) DRL for gener-</h5>
<h5>ating flow migration policies, while maximizing QoS satisfactions and minimizing re-</h5>
<h5>source consumption. DeepMigration demonstrated significant performance improve-</h5>
<h5>ments in network functions virtualization (NFV)-enabled flow migration by reducing the</h5>
<h5>costs and saving up to 71.6% of computation times compared to selected baselines. Spe-</h5>
<h5>cifically, in scale-out scenarios, the proposed framework achieved 63.3% less migration</h5>
<h5>cost than OFM [71].</h5>
<pre><code>Figure 6. GNN and DRL on SDN architecture for routing optimization.
</code></pre>
<h5>Furthermore, He et al. [ 69 ], leveraged knowledge-defined networking architecture for</h5>
<h5>machine learning programmability to transform network data into actionable knowledge</h5>
<h5>that intelligently guides routing policies for improved network management. The proposed</h5>
<h5>integration, message passing DRL (MPDRL) architecture, utilized GNN and DDPG to</h5>
<h5>address routing optimization by (1) GNN interacts with the network topology to extract</h5>
<h5>knowledge through message passing, while (2) DDPG utilizes the hidden knowledge to</h5>
<h5>generate optimal routing policies. In this study, authors optimized the combination for</h5>
<h5>enhancing network traffic load balancing and overall performance by dynamically adapting</h5>
<h5>to network changes. Through extensive experiments across three real-world internet service</h5>
<h5>provider (ISP) network topologies, the proposed routing optimization method consistently</h5>
<h5>achieved better network performance metrics, such as reducing maximum link utilization,</h5>
<h5>decreasing E2E network delay, and enhancing overall network utility compared to (1) open</h5>
<h5>shortest path first, (2) equal-cost multi-path, and (3) the original DDPG methods.</h5>
<h5>4.2.2. Flow Migration</h5>
<h5>Sun et al. [ 70 ] proposed an optimization approach on flow migration, which referred</h5>
<h5>to the dynamic relocation of traffic among different network function instances to adapt the</h5>
<h5>loading statuses and balancing between network service quality and resource utilization</h5>
<h5>efficiency. The proposed framework was termed DeepMigration, which utilized (1) GNN</h5>
<h5>to handle graph-structured topology and flow distribution and (2) DRL for generating</h5>
<h5>flow migration policies, while maximizing QoS satisfactions and minimizing resource</h5>
<h5>consumption. DeepMigration demonstrated significant performance improvements in</h5>
<h5>network functions virtualization (NFV)-enabled flow migration by reducing the costs and</h5>
<h5>saving up to 71.6% of computation times compared to selected baselines. Specifically,</h5>
<h5>in scale-out scenarios, the proposed framework achieved 63.3% less migration cost than</h5>
<h5>OFM [71].</h5>
<h5>4.2.3. Traffic Steering</h5>
<h5>In this sub-section, we focus on the intelligent management and routing of network</h5>
<h5>traffic to optimize the deployment of SFC in SDN/NFV-enabled environments. Rafiq</h5>
<h5>et al. [ 72 ] integrated RouteNet model [ 73 ] with a delay-aware traffic flow steering module</h5>
<h5>for optimal SFC deployment and traffic steering in SDN controller. The proposed scheme</h5>
<h5>predicted optimal paths considering delays through GNN. The system autonomously</h5>
<h5>selected paths with minimal delay for traffic steering and SFC deployment by leveraging</h5>
<h5>the knowledge plane for decision making. As a result, the system demonstrated efficient</h5>
<h5>resource utilization and optimal SFC deployment across different scenarios. For instance,</h5>
<h5>deploying 5 VNFs across separate compute nodes showed the model’s capability in the</h5>
<h5>experiment to efficiently allocate resources, while achieving significant improvements in</h5>
<h5>latency and resource management.</h5>
<h5>Furthermore, in Xiao et al. [ 74 ], SFC mapping was studied by a two-stage DRL frame-</h5>
<h5>work, which integrated on GCN-based proximal policy optimization to address the embed-</h5>
<h5>ding problem in multi-datacenter networks. The first stage provided a macro perspective</h5>
<h5>solution by treating all SFCs in a data center’s queue as a single entity, which aimed for</h5>
<h5>load balancing policies across multi-datacenters. The early stage involved modelling the</h5>
<h5>load transfer as a MDP to optimize SFC placement for maximizing request acceptance</h5>
<h5>while minimizing costs. The second stage refined the early stage by embedding SFCs</h5>
<h5>within each datacenter’s local observation scope, while using a multi-agent reinforcement</h5>
<h5>learning approach to achieve efficient SFC embedding with minimized costs. On average,</h5>
<h5>the framework outperformed Kolin and DQN methods by 13% and 18%, respectively.</h5>
<h5>4.2.4. Dynamic Path Reconfiguration</h5>
<h5>Liu et al. [ 75 ] introduced a novel GNN-based dynamic resource prediction model and</h5>
<h5>deep dyna-Q-based reconfiguration algorithm for optimizing SFC paths in IoT networks.</h5>
<h5>The proposed GNN model was used for forecasting VNF instance resource requirements</h5>
<h5>for facilitating proactive reconfiguration decisions. The system dynamically adapted SFCs</h5>
<h5>based on predicted and real-time data that aim to balance between resources and service</h5>
<h5>performances. The authors addressed the SFC reconfiguration problem by proposing a</h5>
<h5>trade-off optimization between maximizing revenue and minimizing reconfiguration costs,</h5>
<h5>including both migration and bandwidth expenses. Utilizing deep dyna-Q-based method,</h5>
<h5>the study overcome the NP-hard nature of the problem, while integrating with GNN for</h5>
<h5>graph-structured scalability. The effectiveness of the proposed model was validated against</h5>
<h5>exact solutions for small networks. The experimental evaluation demonstrated the model’s</h5>
<h5>effectiveness with an average CPU root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 0.17 on improved</h5>
<h5>GNN, which was significantly lower than 0.75 achieved by original GNN.</h5>
<h5>4.3. Core Networks</h5>
<h5>4.3.1. VNF Optimization</h5>
<h5>By leveraging the virtualization and softwarization from SDN/NFV-based infras-</h5>
<h5>tructure, GNN+DRL can obtain efficient computing capabilities with replay buffer for</h5>
<h5>multi-epoch training towards the optimization of VNF placements, as shown in Figure 7.</h5>
<h5>Sun et al. [ 76 ] proposed a combination of DRL framework with graph network-based neural</h5>
<h5>network for optimal VNF placement, which addresses the challenges of resource constraints</h5>
<h5>in different VNF identifiers and QoS requirements in massive network traffic. The authors</h5>
<h5>proposed DeepOpt architecture to operate within an SDN-enabled environment, where</h5>
<h5>graph network is utilized to generalize network topology (resource, storage, bandwidth,</h5>
<h5>and tolerable delays).</h5>

<h2>requests at 0.22% compared to other conventional approaches.</h2>

<h2>by dynamically adjusting resources in real-time.</h2>

<h5>The DRL framework employed the REINFORCE algorithm to optimize the placement</h5>
<h5>strategy by generating actions (node selections) and calculating rewards based on cost,</h5>
<h5>penalties, and delay factors. The proposed approach ensures minimal resource consump-</h5>
<h5>tion and adherence to QoS constraints, which showcased a low reject ratio of SFC requests</h5>
<h5>at 0.22% compared to other conventional approaches.</h5>
<h5>In terms of optimization, Jalodia et al. [ 77 ] studied resource prediction of VNF with</h5>
<h5>asynchronous DRL enhanced GNN in NFV-enabled system architecture. The authors ad-</h5>
<h5>dressed the complexity of NFV environments by considering the topology of SFC and em-</h5>

<h5>The proposed approach improved prediction accuracy and operational efficiency by dy-</h5>
<h5>namically adjusting resources in real-time.</h5>
<h5>4.3.2. Adaptive SFC</h5>
<h5>Hara et al. [ 78 ] critically considered the high-dimensional changes in graph-structured</h5>
<h5>network topology and service demands that handles future massive service chain requests.</h5>
<h5>In SDN/NFV-enabled environment, authors adopted GNN for approximating the q-values</h5>
<h5>within double DQN framework. The model transformed the network by reinterpreting</h5>
<h5>links as nodes. In this transformed network, nodes are connected if their corresponding</h5>
<h5>links in the original network share a common node, which allows the original network’s</h5>
<h5>link features to be viewed as node features in the transformed network that leveraging</h5>
<h5>the adjacency matrix for analysis. The authors obtained the enhancing key performance</h5>
<h5>indicators on packet drop reduction, average delay reduction, robustness against network</h5>
<h5>topology changes, and optimal response to various hyperparameter settings. Figure 8</h5>
<h5>presents the overview of logical adaptive SFC for slicing applications between high to</h5>
<h5>low-mission-critical.</h5>

<h5>Qi et al. [79] studied graph-structured SFCs and leveraged GCN capabilities to extract</h5>
<h5>the deep hidden states, while representing as Q-networks. By integrating GCN with con-</h5>
<h5>strained DDQN for energy-efficient VNF deployment, authors addressed the SFC chal-</h5>
<h5>lenges by optimizing the E2E delay of SFC request with high successful ratios of deploy-</h5>
<h5>ing VNFs. The proposed approach introduced a mask mechanism in DDQN to ensure that</h5>
<h5>resource constraints are met. In experimental results, the approach outperformed tradi-</h5>
<h5>tional DDQN and greedy algorithms, also achieved better performance in handling un-</h5>
<h5>seen SFC graphs.</h5>
<h5>4.3.3. Core Slicing</h5>
<h5>Tan et al. [80] proposed a novel E2E 5G slice embedding framework that integrates</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL, primarily in core, to dynamically embed network slices. Utilizing a heteroge-</h5>
<h5>neous GNN-based encoder, the scheme captured the complex multidimensional embed-</h5>
<h5>ding environment, including the substrate and slice networks’ topologies and their rela-</h5>
<h5>tionships. A dueling network-based decoder with variable output sizes was employed to</h5>
<h5>generate optimal embedding decisions. The system was trained using the dueling double</h5>
<h5>DQN algorithm, namely D3QN, for enhancing the flexibility and efficiency of slice em-</h5>
<h5>bedding decisions under various traffic conditions and future service requirements. The</h5>
<h5>proposed GNN+DRL integration achieved higher accumulated revenues for mobile net-</h5>
<h5>work operators (MNOs) with moderate embedding costs. Specifically, authors obtained</h5>
<h5>significant improvements in embedding efficiency and cost-effectiveness, which show-</h5>
<h5>cased its potential for practical deployment in 5G and beyond networks.</h5>
<pre><code>Figure 8. GNN+DRL for orchestrating service chains.
</code></pre>
<h5>Qi et al. [ 79 ] studied graph-structured SFCs and leveraged GCN capabilities to ex-</h5>
<h5>tract the deep hidden states, while representing as Q-networks. By integrating GCN with</h5>
<h5>constrained DDQN for energy-efficient VNF deployment, authors addressed the SFC chal-</h5>
<h5>lenges by optimizing the E2E delay of SFC request with high successful ratios of deploying</h5>
<h5>VNFs. The proposed approach introduced a mask mechanism in DDQN to ensure that re-</h5>
<h5>source constraints are met. In experimental results, the approach outperformed traditional</h5>
<h5>DDQN and greedy algorithms, also achieved better performance in handling unseen SFC</h5>
<h5>graphs.</h5>
<h5>4.3.3. Core Slicing</h5>
<h5>Tan et al. [ 80 ] proposed a novel E2E 5G slice embedding framework that integrates</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL, primarily in core, to dynamically embed network slices. Utilizing a heteroge-</h5>
<h5>neous GNN-based encoder, the scheme captured the complex multidimensional embedding</h5>
<h5>environment, including the substrate and slice networks’ topologies and their relation-</h5>
<h5>ships. A dueling network-based decoder with variable output sizes was employed to</h5>
<h5>generate optimal embedding decisions. The system was trained using the dueling dou-</h5>
<h5>ble DQN algorithm, namely D3QN, for enhancing the flexibility and efficiency of slice</h5>
<h5>embedding decisions under various traffic conditions and future service requirements.</h5>
<h5>The proposed GNN+DRL integration achieved higher accumulated revenues for mobile</h5>
<h5>network operators (MNOs) with moderate embedding costs. Specifically, authors obtained</h5>
<h5>significant improvements in embedding efficiency and cost-effectiveness, which showcased</h5>
<h5>its potential for practical deployment in 5G and beyond networks.</h5>
<h5>4.3.4. SLA Management</h5>
<h5>Jalodia et al. [ 81 ] combined graph convolutional recurrent networks for accurate spatio-</h5>
<h5>temporal forecasting of system SLA metrics and deep Q-learning for enforcing dynamic</h5>
<h5>SLA-aware scaling policies. By capturing both spatial and temporal dependencies within</h5>
<h5>the network, the graph convolutional recurrent networks model forecasted potential SLA</h5>
<h5>violations. The deep Q-learning component utilized these forecasts to train on scaling</h5>
<h5>actions, which aimed to optimize for long-term SLA compliance. The proposed approach</h5>
<h5>allowed for proactive management of network resources, while reducing the risk of SLA</h5>
<h5>breaches and enhancing overall network efficiency. The proposed framework achieved</h5>
<h5>a 74.62% improvement in forecasting performance over the baseline approaches, which</h5>
<h5>demonstrated better prediction accuracy for preventing SLA violations.</h5>
<h5>5. Application Deployment Scenarios</h5>
<h5>In this section, we address the collaboration between GNN and DRL to enable efficient</h5>
<h5>technique of applications in three deployment scenarios, namely smart transportation,</h5>
<h5>smart factory, and smart grids, for improving overall autonomy, scalability, and efficiency.</h5>
<h5>Figure 9 illustrates the overview of applied models for efficient smart services.</h5>

<h5>tial SLA violations. The deep Q-learning component utilized these forecasts to train on</h5>
<h5>scaling actions, which aimed to optimize for long-term SLA compliance. The proposed</h5>
<h5>approach allowed for proactive management of network resources, while reducing the</h5>

<h5>5.1. Smart Transportation</h5>
<h5>In [ 82 ], the authors address the complexity of V2X communications from the perspec-</h5>
<h5>tive of task allocation, which can be processed either locally or by an MEC server. The</h5>
<h5>authors identified communication scenarios as a significant aspect of channel conditions</h5>
<h5>in MIMO-NOMA-based V2I communications. The paper proposed a decentralized DRL</h5>
<h5>approach for power allocation in the vehicular edge computing (VEC) model that enhanced</h5>
<h5>optimal policy of DDPG in terms of power consumption and reward improvement. Further-</h5>
<h5>more, [ 42 ] employed DQN to learn the optimal value for the V2X pair, which considered</h5>
<h5>the agent within the RL framework in terms of action and resource allocation observation.</h5>
<h5>The authors of [ 60 ] discussed the utilization of VNF forwarding graph embedding to enable</h5>
<h5>network services while formalizing VNF forwarding graph allocation problems as MDs</h5>
<h5>solved by DRL to simplify the complexity of management and orchestration in telecom-</h5>
<h5>munication network service deployment. The study integrated heuristic algorithms and</h5>
<h5>DDPG to enhance exploration in DRL agents.</h5>
<h5>Beyond that, [ 83 ] examined unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) use in bolstering IoT edge</h5>
<h5>network performance, addressing challenges such as limited computational capacity and</h5>
<h5>energy availability. The authors proposed a multi-agent DRL-based strategy to minimize</h5>
<h5>network computation costs while upholding the QoS of IoT devices. The authors of [ 84 ]</h5>
<h5>introduced innovative solutions in IoV by employing a novel DRL method for the vehicle</h5>
<h5>handover process, which aims to reduce handover failures. The paper also suggested</h5>
<h5>a fuzzy-based GNN to navigate the network selection problem by incorporating fuzzy</h5>
<h5>logic into the graph structure. The authors of [ 85 ] explored challenges in developing</h5>
<h5>vehicular ad hoc networks and proposed DRL to optimize network caching and computing.</h5>
<h5>Additionally, [ 86 ] outlined an efficient path planning scheme for UAVs in data gathering,</h5>
<h5>which addressed scenarios without communication infrastructure by leveraging DRL for</h5>
<h5>planning UAV hover points. A cluster-head searching algorithm with an autonomous</h5>
<h5>exploration pattern was utilized to adapt to fluctuating positions. The authors of [ 87 ]</h5>
<h5>focused on enhancing taxi service demand and providers’ profits through GNN models,</h5>
<h5>which address scalability challenges by proposing a heterogeneous GNN-LSTM algorithm.</h5>
<h5>Figure 10 illustrates how BS and UAVs can be managed by core controller (using GNN+DRL</h5>
<h5>modelling) to enhance the coverage with fault tolerance in smart transportation.</h5>

<h5>5.2. Smart Factory</h5>
<h5>This section highlights the potential of GNNs and DRL in revolutionizing smart factory</h5>
<h5>operations through intelligent decision making, efficient resource utilization, and adaptive</h5>
<h5>control of manufacturing process. In [ 88 ], authors presented a DRL-based decentralized</h5>
<h5>computation offloading method tailored for intelligent manufacturing scenarios. The paper</h5>
<h5>introduced the dual-critic DDPG algorithm that uses two-critic networks to accelerate</h5>
<h5>the convergence process and minimize computational costs in edge computing systems.</h5>
<h5>By implementing a multi-user system model with a single-edge server, the dual-critic</h5>
<h5>DDPG algorithm efficiently addresses computation offloading and resource allocation</h5>
<h5>challenges while demonstrating good performance in reducing system computational costs</h5>
<h5>for intensive tasks in smart factory.</h5>
<h5>In [ 89 ], authors presented an interesting software-defined factory architecture with</h5>
<h5>DRL-based QoS optimization. Double DQN approach was proposed to manage network</h5>
<h5>load balance and dynamic traffic scheduling while meeting low-latency requirements. The</h5>
<h5>method analyzed QoS threshold and focused on latency/bandwidth by utilizing double</h5>
<h5>DQN to find optimal data flow paths. The proposed architecture included layers for</h5>
<h5>interoperating heterogeneous networks, SDN centralized control, and DRL agents, which</h5>
<h5>demonstrated improved latency, jitter, and throughput that offered a smart solution for</h5>
<h5>dynamic traffic management in smart factories.</h5>
<h5>Moreover, in [ 90 ], authors presented a GNN-based approach with the time-series</h5>
<h5>similarity-based GAT for predicting cellular traffic. By utilizing dynamic time warping and</h5>
<h5>graph attention mechanisms, the proposed method effectively captured spatial-temporal</h5>
<h5>relationships in cellular data, which enhanced prediction accuracy for smart factory envi-</h5>
<h5>ronments. In [ 91 ], authors proposed a GNN-enhanced DQN algorithm for dynamic QoS</h5>
<h5>flow path allocation in smart factory networks while addressing heterogeneous network</h5>
<h5>challenges. The proposed method optimized traffic management by learning network</h5>
<h5>states and allocation strategies, which improved agent learning efficiency with prioritized</h5>
<h5>experience replay under sparse reward conditions. The authors showcased adaptability to</h5>
<h5>network topology changes and autonomous operation in smart factory.</h5>
<h5>5.3. Smart Grids</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL offers significant opportunities to enhance smart grid reliability, efficiency,</h5>
<h5>and sustainability, moving towards more intelligent and resilient energy systems. By point-</h5>
<h5>ing out potential challenges (e.g., various QoS levels including periodic fixed scheduling</h5>
<h5>and emergency-driven packets), traditional smart grids struggle with adaptability to mas-</h5>
<h5>sive/congested network conditions and adhere QoS requirements. In [ 92 ], the authors</h5>
<h5>discussed an SDN proactive routing solution using GNN for improved traffic prediction.</h5>
<h5>The paper targeted on improving QoS by (1) predicting future network congestion using</h5>
<h5>GNN and (2) dynamically adjusting routing paths and queue service rates through DRL.</h5>
<h5>The proposed method enhanced the smart grid proactivity in handling of regular and</h5>
<h5>emergency data traffic, which showcased an innovative approach to managing network</h5>
<h5>resources and ensuring service delivery under peak and off-peak conditions.</h5>
<h5>In [ 93 ], authors focused on evaluating the cyber layer in systems, such as IEEE 14-bus</h5>
<h5>and IEEE 39-bus test systems, where accurately predicting traffic demands is essential</h5>
<h5>for efficient resource allocation by proposing a GNN and LSTM method for capturing</h5>
<h5>spatial-temporal traffic patterns. By collecting data on 5G transmissions through power</h5>
<h5>private LTE-G networks, including terminal locations and bandwidth requirements, the</h5>
<h5>method used (1) LSTM to model temporal correlations and (2) GNN to capture spatial</h5>
<h5>relationships between BS. The dual approach allows for accurate predictions of BS traffic,</h5>
<h5>which supports efficient resource allocation and network optimization in smart grids.</h5>
<h5>Moreover, [ 94 ] highlighted the integration of green IoT within smart grid systems</h5>
<h5>while addressing resource allocation in RAN slicing and mapping these challenges to DRL</h5>
<h5>for enhanced flexibility and service delivery. And [ 95 ] aimed to solve energy efficiency and</h5>
<h5>delay challenges in heterogeneous cellular networks for various task delay requirements,</h5>
<h5>which suggested DRL approach for learning optimal policies based on predicted SINR</h5>
<h5>states for enhancing decision making in executing high successful transmissions. The</h5>
<h5>proposed approach dynamically adjusted power levels and access strategies to balance</h5>
<h5>the trade-off between minimizing energy consumption and meeting the delay constraints</h5>
<pre><code>Electronics 2024 , 13 , 994 23 of 29
</code></pre>
<h5>of smart grid data traffic, which demonstrated a practical approach to improving the</h5>
<h5>performance of cellular networks and supporting real-world smart grid applications.</h5>
<h5>6. Potential Challenges and Future Directions</h5>
<h5>To the best of our knowledge, we point out these challenges as a potential guide,</h5>
<h5>which we believe to pinpoint the primary directions for advancing GNN+DRL solutions</h5>
<h5>that are not only highly efficient but also trustworthy, adaptable, and verifiable for ensuring</h5>
<h5>the long-term viability in the ever-evolving optimization algorithms of handling future</h5>
<h5>massive data traffic and merging towards network automation solutions. There are four</h5>
<h5>primary challenges and directions as follows.</h5>
<h5>6.1. Explainable GNN+DRL</h5>
<h5>While integration offers remarkable potential, granularity and complexity present a</h5>
<h5>significant challenge, particularly, when these models deploy in critical infrastructure, the</h5>
<h5>decision-making hypothesis becomes increasingly concerned and requires deep inspection.</h5>
<h5>The interpretable GNN architectures require further explorations that inherently reveal</h5>
<h5>the reasons behind each flow-level, node-level, and graph-level predictions (including</h5>
<h5>attention mechanisms or layer-wise explanations). Beyond architecture interpretation,</h5>
<h5>future studies should enable or guide users to understand how altering inputs would affect</h5>
<h5>model outputs, which fosters trust and debugging capabilities. Moreover, researchers can</h5>
<h5>extend by developing methods to extract insights from pre-trained models. Addressing</h5>
<h5>explainability is not only ethically necessary but also crucial for regulatory compliance and</h5>
<h5>gaining wider adoption in safety-critical domains. Figure 11 describes how explainable</h5>
<h5>modelling interacts to stakeholders with understanding interfaces and outputs.</h5>
<p><em>Electronics</em> <strong>2024</strong> , <em>13</em> , x FOR PEER REVIEW 29 of 29</p>
<h4>constraints of smart grid data traffic, which demonstrated a practical approach to improv-</h4>
<h4>ing the performance of cellular networks and supporting real-world smart grid applica-</h4>
<h4>tions.</h4>
<h4>6. Potential Challenges and Future Directions</h4>

<h4>6.1. Explainable GNN+DRL</h4>
<h4>While integration offers remarkable potential, granularity and complexity present a</h4>
<h4>significant challenge, particularly, when these models deploy in critical infrastructure, the</h4>
<h4>decision-making hypothesis becomes increasingly concerned and requires deep inspec-</h4>

<h4>veal the reasons behind each flow-level, node-level, and graph-level predictions (includ-</h4>
<h4>ing attention mechanisms or layer-wise explanations). Beyond architecture interpretation,</h4>
<h4>future studies should enable or guide users to understand how altering inputs would af-</h4>

<h4>can extend by developing methods to extract insights from pre-trained models. Address-</h4>

<h4>Therefore, in future studies, there are two essential aspects that can be addressed,</h4>
<h4>including (1) attention mechanisms, which highlights the specific network features influ-</h4>
<h4>encing GNN outputs that can provide insights into decision rationale, and (2) simulation-</h4>
<h4>based explanations, which illustrates how different network states would affect DRL ac-</h4>
<h4>tion selections and reward evaluations.</h4>
<h4>6.2. Overhead Consumption: Latency, Energy and Computing</h4>
<h4>The computational demands of GNN+DRL raise concerns about its real-world ap-</h4>
<h4>plicability. Beyond formulating reward functions that jointly consider latency, energy,</h4>
<h4>and computing resources, future research should focus on:</h4>

<h5>Therefore, in future studies, there are two essential aspects that can be addressed,</h5>
<h5>including (1) attention mechanisms, which highlights the specific network features influenc-</h5>
<h5>ing GNN outputs that can provide insights into decision rationale, and (2) simulation-based</h5>
<h5>explanations, which illustrates how different network states would affect DRL action selec-</h5>
<h5>tions and reward evaluations.</h5>
<h5>6.2. Overhead Consumption: Latency, Energy and Computing</h5>
<h5>The computational demands of GNN+DRL raise concerns about its real-world appli-</h5>
<h5>cability. Beyond formulating reward functions that jointly consider latency, energy, and</h5>
<h5>computing resources, future research should focus on:</h5>
<pre><code>Electronics 2024 , 13 , 994 24 of 29
</code></pre>
<ul>
<li><strong>Lightweight GNN architectures</strong> , which designs efficient GNNs with reduced pa-</li>
</ul>
<h5>rameter counts and computational complexity, potentially leveraging knowledge</h5>
<h5>distillation or pruning techniques.</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Hardware acceleration</strong> , which explores specialized hardware (e.g., GPUs, TPUs)</li>
</ul>
<h5>or hardware-software co-design to accelerate GNN computations and enable (near)</h5>
<h5>real-time capability.</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Model compression and quantization</strong> , which reduces model size and memory foot-</li>
</ul>
<h5>print while maintaining accuracy. Therefore, we allow the deployment on network</h5>
<h5>architecture of resource-constrained devices. By optimizing GNN+DRL for efficiency,</h5>
<h5>we can obtain the benefits for ensuring its practical viability in latency-sensitive and</h5>
<h5>energy-aware industrial IoT applications.</h5>
<h5>6.3. Interoperability with Existing Schemes</h5>
<h5>Integrating GNN+DRL with existing network infrastructure presents a significant</h5>
<h5>challenge. The key research directions include (1) hybrid approaches, which combines</h5>
<h5>with traditional network protocols and architectures (e.g., SDN, NFV, MEC) for enabling a</h5>
<h5>gradual transition and leveraging existing operations, (2) standardized interfaces, which</h5>
<h5>defines open and adaptable interfaces that allow GNN+DRL models to seamlessly interact</h5>
<h5>with diverse network components and protocols, and (3) backward compatibility, which</h5>
<h5>ensures that new models can work with older systems (minimizing disruption and facili-</h5>
<h5>tating wider adoption). Figure 12 illustrates the overview of interoperating GNN+DRL in</h5>
<h5>existing software-defined and virtualized infrastructures.</h5>

</ul>
<h5>rameter counts and computational complexity, potentially leveraging knowledge</h5>

<ul>

<h5>ciency, we can obtain the benefits for ensuring its practical viability in latency-sensi-</h5>
<h5>tive and energy-aware industrial IoT applications.</h5>
<h5>6.3. Interoperability with Existing Schemes</h5>
<h5>Integrating GNN+DRL with existing network infrastructure presents a significant</h5>
<h5>challenge. The key research directions include (1) hybrid approaches, which combines</h5>
<h5>with traditional network protocols and architectures (e.g., SDN, NFV, MEC) for enabling</h5>
<h5>a gradual transition and leveraging existing operations, (2) standardized interfaces, which</h5>
<h5>defines open and adaptable interfaces that allow GNN+DRL models to seamlessly interact</h5>
<h5>with diverse network components and protocols, and (3) backward compatibility, which</h5>
<h5>ensures that new models can work with older systems (minimizing disruption and facili-</h5>
<h5>tating wider adoption). Figure 12 illustrates the overview of interoperating GNN+DRL in</h5>
<h5>existing software-defined and virtualized infrastructures.</h5>
<pre><code>Figure 12. Figure 12. Interoperability of GNN+DRL with SDN,Interoperability of GNN+DRL with SDN, NFV, MEC, and federated learning. NFV, MEC, and federated learning.
</code></pre>
<h5>Addressing interoperability is crucial for ensuring a smooth transition towards high-</h5>
<h5>applicable GNN+DRL-powered networks and maximizing the benefits of these paradigms.</h5>
<h5>Researchers can develop common formats for GNN representations that facilitate interop-</h5>
<h5>erability with diverse network protocols or build adapter modules that translate between</h5>
<h5>GNN+DRL models and existing network protocols. Beyond these, federated learning can</h5>
<h5>be a good choice for integration to offer collaborative training across different network</h5>
<h5>architectures; therefore, creating interoperable models without compromising each network</h5>
<h5>privacy [96,97].</h5>
<h5>6.4. Reproducibility Awareness</h5>
<h5>The diverse and complex requirements of future digital networks necessitate robust</h5>
<h5>reproducibility practices in GNN+DRL research. Building a strong foundation of repro-</h5>
<h5>ducibility is essential for fostering research growth in GNN+DRL and ensuring its practical</h5>
<h5>impact. The key research areas include:</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Building standardized benchmarks and datasets</strong> , which develop publicly available,</li>
</ul>
<h5>well-documented datasets and benchmarks that represent real-world network scenar-</h5>
<h5>ios; therefore, enabling consistent evaluation and comparison across different studies.</h5>
<h5>Due to a lack of comprehensive studies or data across all domains (access, transport,</h5>
<h5>and core networks), researchers face several issues to conduct the comparison and</h5>
<h5>identify the key metrics to target during experimentation. Different studies may use</h5>
<h5>varied metrics, which making direct comparisons challenging.</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Code and model sharing</strong> , which encourage open-source code and model sharing to</li>
</ul>
<h5>facilitate collaboration, reproducibility, and accelerate research progress.</h5>
<ul>
<li><strong>Experimental design guidelines</strong> , which establish best practices for experimental de-</li>
</ul>
<h5>sign, data collection, and model evaluation to ensure the validity and generalizability</h5>
<h5>of the research findings.</h5>
<h5>7. Conclusions</h5>
<h5>Our survey has delved into the integrating potential of GNN and DRL within E2E</h5>
<h5>networking policies. We have showcased how GNN proficiency in encoding large-scale</h5>
<h5>network topologies and DRL adaptability in continuous learning and agent decisions in</h5>
<h5>order to pave the way for innovative network solutions. From optimizing access networks</h5>
<h5>(RAN slicing, resource allocation, user association, and cluster-free NOMA) to enhancing</h5>
<h5>transport networks (routing optimization, flow migration, traffic steering, and dynamic</h5>
<h5>path reconfiguration), and improving core networks (VNF optimization, adaptive SFC,</h5>
<h5>core slicing, and SLA management), the integrated GNN and DRL framework stands as</h5>
<h5>one of the key backbone algorithms for activating zero-touch network automation. Deploy-</h5>
<h5>ments in smart transportation, smart factory, and smart grids demonstrate the practical</h5>
<h5>benefits of these advancements. However, potential challenges in ensuring explainability,</h5>
<h5>handling overhead consumption, achieving interoperability, and promoting reproducibility</h5>
<h5>awareness appear as frontiers for future exploration. As our conclusion, pointing out</h5>
<h5>these directions will ensure that GNN+DRL solutions remain robust and applicable in the</h5>
<h5>dynamic aspects of future massive network states.</h5>
